narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your LSAT score of 35 also sits a bit under the average of around 37 that we see in successful applicants, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is just slightly below the usual 3.21. Coming from a middle-income family aligns with many applicants, and as a white male planning full-time studies, your demographics match the majority in our dataset. When the model weighed all this, your third-year grade decile had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, followed closely by your first-year decile, then your LSAT score. Your family income level came next in influence, nudging the prediction somewhat toward failure, with your racial background and undergraduate GPA playing smaller but similar roles. Your full-time status and gender had the least impact, still leaning slightly against passing. Overall, these factors together tipped the scales just enough to forecast a risk of not passing the bar right away, unlike typical students whose stronger early and later grades, higher LSATs, and balanced profiles predict success more reliably. The good news is you can take steps to shift this outlook—focus on retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's something many improve with targeted prep. Building stronger academic habits now could help if you pursue further education or certifications that demonstrate growth beyond your past deciles. Your family income and demographics are harder to change, but excelling in any upcoming coursework or bar prep courses could signal to future evaluators that you're overcoming those early hurdles. With dedication, you can realistically strengthen these key areas and reapply stronger next time. Keep pushing forward—you've got the potential to turn this around.
